content.problemStatement:
In this worked out example, we have created a Book Catalog App that allows users to:
- Add a new book with a title, author, and price
- View a list of all books that have been added

Here we can see how forms, models, and views work together in Django!

#### models.py – The Database Structure
This file defines the Book model, which represents the kind of data you want to store. \
This tells Django to create a database table with three columns: title, author, and price.

#### forms.py – The Form Interface
This file creates a form (based on the Book model) that users can fill out. \
`class BookForm(forms.ModelForm)`
- You're creating a form class called BookForm.
- This form inherits from Django's built-in ModelForm class.
- ModelForm is a shortcut that lets you automatically generate a form based on a database model (in this case, the Book model).

`class Meta`
- This is a special inner class called Meta that tells Django how to build the form.
- Meta is like the form’s settings/configuration.

`model = Book` \
This tells Django: \
“Use the Book model to create the form fields.” \
Django will look at the Book model and generate corresponding form fields for its attributes (title, author, and price).

`fields = ['title', 'author', 'price']` \
This line specifies which fields from the model you want to include in the form. \
Django will only create input fields for these three.

#### views.py – The Logic
- `add_book_view`: shows the form and saves a new book when submitted (Renders the template `add_book.html`)
- `thank_you_view`: displays a confirmation message (Renders the template `thank_you.html`)
- `book_list_view`: displays a list of all books added so far (Renders the template `book_list.html`)

The URLs are also set up like how we usually do. \
Click on **RUN** which will make migrations and run the server. \
See the form that opens up, add data to it and view the data.
